title: Driver lines for studying associative learning in Drosophila

top_institutions.label: The University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center

match.title: Driver lines for studying associative learning in Drosophila.

elife_assessment.summary: This important collection of over 800 new cell type-specific driver lines will be an invaluable resource for researchers studying associative learning in Drosophila. Thoroughly characterized and well documented, this collection will permit researchers to selectively target neurons that deliver information to, or receive it from, the memory center of the fly brain called the Mushroom Body. Given the wealth of new drivers and the genetic access they provide to over 300 cell types, this compelling work will be of interest not only to researchers studying the mechanisms of associative learning but more generally to those dissecting sensorimotor circuits in the fly nervous system.

narrative:
Short impact summary

The article "Driver lines for studying associative learning in Drosophila," published in eLife, represents a significant contribution to the field of neurobiology, particularly in the study of associative learning mechanisms in fruit flies. Supported by prominent institutions such as the Howard Hughes Medical Institute and the Technion – Israel Institute of Technology, this work highlights critical insights and demonstrates a robust engagement with the academic community through citations and early sharing as a preprint.

Full narrative

This research article has garnered a notable citation count of 26 since its publication, indicating its relevance and the impact it is having on ongoing discussions in neuroscience. The strong institutional affiliations, including the University of Michigan and Janelia Research Campus, lend additional credibility and support to the findings presented in this study.

Furthermore, the authors are commended for their proactive approach in sharing an early version of their research as a preprint, which has already seen 15 citations. This practice of open science not only facilitates immediate engagement with the scientific community but also allows for a quicker dissemination of new ideas and findings, positioning this work at the forefront of contemporary research in the field.

The inclusion of 12 Research Resource Identifiers (RRIDs) demonstrates the authors' strong commitment to technical reproducibility. By providing these identifiers, the researchers ensure that others can access and verify the resources used in their study, thus enhancing the credibility and reliability of their findings.

Moreover, the article has been supported by proven downstream reuse, indicating that the materials and data derived from this research are actively contributing to further studies in the field. The combination of strong institutional backing, early engagement through preprints, and a commitment to resource transparency underscores the significant role this research plays in advancing our understanding of associative learning in Drosophila.
